---
title: "2.9 Implementing Selection and Iteration Algorithms"
subtitle: "Part 3: Digits, Remainder, Math.random, and Frequency"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Finding Digits

### Use `/` and `%` with 10

We can also use division (`/`) and remainder (`%`) to find the digits of a number by dividing by `10`.

For example, `1234` divided by `10` is `123` with a remainder of `4`.

In integer division the result truncates everything to the right of the decimal point, so we get the number without the last digit when we divide by `10`. The remainder is the last digit of the number.

## Digit Algorithms

### Reuse the same idea

You could use this algorithm to add the digits of a number, reverse the digits of a number, or check if a digit is equal to a certain number.

::: {.tight-list}
- `num % 10` gives the rightmost digit
- `num / 10` removes the rightmost digit when `num` is an integer
- a loop can repeat those two steps while digits remain
:::

## Code Task

### `activecode:: checkDigit`

Textbook prompt: Complete the `checkDigit` method below to determine if a positive number has a given single-digit number as one of its digits.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: checkDigit`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class CheckDigitTest
{
      // checkDigit checks whether a number has a given digit in it
      public static boolean checkDigit(int number, int digit)
      {
          // 1. Use a while loop: while number is greater than 0

             // 2. Get the last digit of number with % 10
             // 3. if it equals the argument digit, return true
             // 4. remove the last digit of number with / 10

        // if loop finishes, return false
        return false; // Digit not found
    }


    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(checkDigit(12345, 3)); // true
        System.out.println(checkDigit(9876, 2));  // false
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: checkDigit`

Runestone checks that:

::: {.tight-list}
- the code contains a `while` loop
- `main` prints `true` and `false`
- `checkDigit(13579, 7)` returns `true`
- the code contains `number % 10`
:::

## Remainder Uses

### Keep `%` in mind

The <span class="term">remainder</span> operator has been used quite a bit on the AP CSA exam.

::: {.tight-list}
- Use remainder to check for odd or even numbers.
- Use remainder to check if any number is evenly divisible by any other.
- Use it to get the last digit from an integer number.
:::

## Remainder Uses

### Hours, minutes, and wraparound

```java
int totalMinutes = 345;
int hours = totalMinutes / 60;   // Number of whole hours, i.e. 5
int minutes = totalMinutes % 60; // Number of minutes left over, i.e. 45
```

Use `%` whenever you have a limit in the value, and you need to wrap around to zero if the value goes over the limit.

The value of `num % limit` will always be in the range from `0` inclusive to `limit` exclusive as long as `num` and `limit` are both positive.

## Math.random in if Statements

### Random ranges represent probabilities

The `Math.random()` method returns a random number between `0.0` and `1.0`.

You can use this method with `if` statements to simulate a coin flip or an event occuring a certain percentage of the time.

```java
if (Math.random() < 0.5)
{
    System.out.println("Heads");
}
else
{
    System.out.println("Tails");
}
```

## Math.random Example

### 90 percent of the time

If you want to simulate an event occuring 90% of the time, you can check the random number to see if it is less than `0.9`.

```java
if (Math.random() < 0.9)
{
    // 90% of the time
    System.out.println("Event happened");
}
else
{
    // 10% of the time
    System.out.println("Event did not happen");
}
```

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: mcq-rnd-ifs`

The weather report says there is approximately 25% chance of rain today.

Which of the following `if` statements would print Rain or No Rain to simulate a day with the correct percentages following the weather report?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `if (Math.random() < 0.25) { System.out.println("Rain"); }`
- B. `if (Math.random() > 0.75) { System.out.println("Rain"); }`
- C. `if (Math.random() > 0.25) { System.out.println("Rain"); }`
- D. `if (Math.random() < 0.75) { System.out.println("No Rain"); }`
:::

## Answer Reasoning

### `mchoice:: mcq-rnd-ifs`

Correct choices from Runestone:

::: {.tight-list}
- A: this code will print `Rain` 25% of the time.
- B: this code will print `Rain` 25%, because `1 - 0.75 = 0.25`.
- D: this code will print `No Rain` 75% of the time, so it will rain 25% of the time.
:::

Choice C prints `Rain` 75% of the time.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: randomShapes`

Textbook prompt: Add an if/else statement that uses `Math.random()` to do a coin flip to decide whether to call `yertle.turnRight()` or `yertle.turnLeft`.

Run the code to see the turtle draw a random shape.

Datafile: `turtleClasses.jar`

## Code Window

### `activecode:: randomShapes`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class RandomTurns
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        World world = new World(500,400);
        Turtle yertle = new Turtle(world);

        // This is a loop that runs 10 times to draw a random shape
        for(int i = 1; i <= 10; i++)
        {
            yertle.forward(20);

            // Write an if/else statement that uses
            // Math.random() to do a coin flip (50%) to choose
            // between yertle.turnRight() or turnLeft()






        } // end of loop
        world.show(true);
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: randomShapes`

Runestone checks that the submitted code contains:

::: {.tight-list}
- `if`
- `else`
- `Math.random()`
- `.5`
:::

## Frequency

### Counter inside a loop

You can use a counter variable inside a loop to determine the frequency with which a specific criteria is met.

For example, you can count the number of even numbers in a sequence of numbers.

```java
int count = 0;
for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)
{
    if (i % 2 == 0)
    {
        count++;
    }
}
System.out.println("There are " + count + " even numbers from 1 to 100.");
```

## Frequency and Boolean Methods

### Use a method as the criterion

The AP exam often gives a boolean method for students to use to determine some criteria in a set of values.

For example, in the following challenge, you can count the number of prime numbers from `1` to `100` given a boolean method to use `isPrime(n)` which returns `true` or `false`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- use `% 10` to get the rightmost digit of an integer
- use `/ 10` to remove the rightmost digit during integer division
- identify which `Math.random()` inequalities represent a given probability
- keep Turtle random-turn code inside the existing loop
- increment a frequency counter only when a criterion is met
- use a boolean method such as `isPrime(n)` as the condition for a frequency count
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 4

[Next: Prime Number Finder →](2-9-part-4-prime-number-finder.html)
